critique.summary: The article criticizes a PC Gamer webpage for its excessive advertising and large data usage, highlighting that the page initially loads 37MB and continues to download more ads, accumulating nearly half a gigabyte in a few minutes. It also mentions the usefulness of RSS readers in avoiding such intrusive content.
mainPoints: The PC Gamer article page displays multiple popups and at least five visible ads upon loading.
The initial page load size is approximately 37MB, which is unusually large for a web article.
The website continues to download additional ads over time, reaching nearly half a gigabyte within five minutes.
RSS readers like NetNewsWire, Unread, Current, and Reeder can help users bypass such intrusive advertising and large data downloads.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the page loading multiple ads and popups are plausible and consistent with common web practices on ad-supported sites. The reported 37MB initial load size and continued downloading of ads totaling nearly half a gigabyte are unusually high but technically possible, especially if the site loads many high-resolution images, videos, or scripts. However, without direct measurement or corroboration from independent sources, these specific data size claims cannot be fully verified. The recommendation of RSS readers as a way to avoid such issues is accurate and widely recognized.
considerations: The article does not provide technical details or tools used to measure the data usage, which would strengthen the claims.
It does not discuss the potential reasons for such high data usage, such as video ads or auto-playing media.
The article does not consider user settings or ad blockers that might mitigate the problem.
No perspectives from PC Gamer or the website operators are included to explain or respond to these issues.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the excessive use of ads and tracking scripts on modern websites, the impact on data usage, and the importance of ad-blocking tools and alternative RSS readers to improve user experience and privacy.

comments.author: kelvinjps10
comments.text: The person who wrote the article and the people in charge of the site are different.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the common disconnect between content creators and site management, which can lead to excessive advertising practices.

comments.author: wing-_-nuts
comments.text:
This reminds me of the bug I experienced on gcp console with dark reader. Somehow this caused a memory leak, and one could watch the page slowly consume GBs of memory. I once came back from lunch to see it had eaten all ram on my MBP and had consumed a massive amount of swap.

I would not have expected a 'dark mode' extension to cause that.
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant example of how browser extensions can cause significant resource issues, illustrating the broader problem of poorly optimized web content.

comments.author: clircle
comments.text: +1 for FreshRSS (recommended at the bottom of the PC Gamer article). I just started my mass migration to self-hosting (it's way better in 2026 than it used to be), and I'm very pleased with the FreshRSS webapp and NetNewsWire integration. I consider it a solid hedge against enshittification. I probably won't go full self-hosting, but I'm enjoying the move.
comments.analysis: Supports the idea of using self-hosted RSS readers as a way to avoid ad-heavy content, offering a practical alternative.

comments.author: charwhee
comments.text: I immediately thought of this article: https://growtika.com/blog/tech-media-collapse . These websites are losing market share to search and AI; clinging to their business model I suspect they are forced to display an inordinate amount of advertising to make up for their dwindling views. The value they provide is still there, but the advertising revenue that paid for it is not.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the economic pressures on content sites leading to increased advertising and reduced user experience.

comments.author: arkt8
comments.text:
I have no metrics but there is a lot (if not most of) sites with similar issues.

A simple site of lyrics, or newspapers that start videos automatically. Github was worse, now at least opens a bit more faster, but still very poorer than, example, codeberg. Sites are sites, most want to do fancy things more than to simply let user read its contents.

Would be nice a site that could track it to put some shame. By now, the better sites are just like HN, Wikipedia... unobstrusive and fast even without cache.
comments.analysis: Highlights the trend of bloated websites with auto-playing media, contrasting with more lightweight, user-friendly alternatives.
